name: StrTok
javadoc:
/** * Provides a somewhat simpler interface for tokenizing strings than * does StreamTokenizer. All tokenizing is done by StreamTokenizer.

* * The major difference from StreamTokenizer is that all tokens are * returned as strings. EOF returns a null, EOL returns an empty string. * A delimiter is returned as a one-character string. Words and numbers * are returned as strings. Quoted strings are also returned as strings * including their quote characters (so they can easily be differentiated * from words and numbers). * * Other differences are:

    *
  • Automatic setup for tokenizing strings. *
  • Underscores are included in identifiers (words). *
  • I/O errors (which should be impossible when tokenizing strings) are * converted to RuntimeExceptions so that every call doesn't have to * be included in a try block. *
  • Convenience functions isWord(), isQString(), and need(). *
  • Returned string tokens are interned for easier comparisons. *
*/

members.name: nextToken
members.javadoc:
/**
 * Returns the next token as a string.  EOF returns a null, EOL
 * returns an empty string.  Delimiters are returned as one character
 * strings.  Quoted strings and words are returned as strings.
 * @return the next token
 */

members.javadoc:
/**
 * Reads the next token and checks that it matches tok.  If it does
 * not match, calls the current error handling routine (see
 * {@link #set_error_handler(StrTok.ErrorHandler) set_error_handler()}).
 * If it does match, just returns.
 * @param tok string to check next token against
 */

tags.name: tok
tags.content: string to check next token against

members.name: need_word
members.javadoc:
/**
 * Reads the next token and checks to make sure that it is a word (id).
 * If it is not a word, calls the error handling routine.  If it is,
 * returns the string of the word.
 * @return next token, if if it a word; otherwise calls the error handling routine
 */

tags.name: next
tags.content: token, if if it a word; otherwise calls the error handling routine
